title: Futures and forwards. Locking in the future
subtitle: Futures and forwards eliminate price uncertainty for both buyer and seller. The obligation to transact is what distinguishes them from options.

body.content: Options give you a right. Futures give you an obligation. That's the core difference, when  you enter a futures contract, you are agreeing to buy or sell a specific asset at a specific price on a specific date, and you must follow through, neither  party can simply walk away.
The original use case: corn in August

A farmer plants corn in May. It'll be ready to sell in September, in  May, corn is at $5 per bushel, the  farmer has bills to pay and needs certainty, they  sell futures: agree to deliver 10,000 bushels in September at $5. A cereal company needs corn in September and doesn't want to bet on prices either, they  buy those same futures, in  September, corn might be $3 or $7, but both parties transact at $5, both  eliminated their price risk, neither  speculated, both  won.

body.label: Mark to market: the daily settlement
body.content: Unlike options (where you pay the premium and wait), futures are settled daily, if  the price moves against your position today, cash leaves your account today, if  it moves in your favor, cash arrives today, this  prevents large losses from silently accumulating, but it also means you can get wiped out quickly if you're leveraged and wrong, if  your account balance falls below the 'maintenance margin' level, you get a margin call: deposit more cash or your position is closed.

Futures trade on exchanges with standardized contracts (specific quantities, dates, specifications). They're liquid, you can exit before expiration. Forwards are private agreements between two parties with customized terms. More flexible, but you're stuck with whoever you made the deal with, there's no exchange to sell to. Forwards carry counterparty risk: if the other party can't pay, you have a problem.

items: Airlines: buy oil futures to lock in fuel costs and protect profit margins from crude price spikes
Gold miners: sell gold futures to lock in revenue before extracting the metal
Agricultural companies: farmers, food processors, grain elevators. The original users
Institutional investors: use index futures to quickly adjust equity exposure without trading hundreds of individual stocks
Banks and pension funds: use interest rate futures to manage duration risk in bond portfolios

body.content: Futures require only a fraction of the contract value as margin, often 5-10%, that  means you control $100,000 of exposure with $5,000-10,000 in capital. A 5% adverse move wipes out your entire margin, this  is the attraction for speculators and the danger for everyone else, the  daily mark-to-market means there's no waiting for recovery, losses are real and immediate.

body.label: Real talk
body.content: Most individual investors have no business trading futures for speculation, the  leverage is extreme, the daily settlement is unforgiving, and the mechanics require constant monitoring, the  people who use futures well are professionals with specific commercial or portfolio hedging needs, not retail investors making directional bets on commodity prices. Know what you are before you enter this market.

Futures and forwards lock in a price for a future transaction, creating certainty for both buyer and seller, the  obligation to transact is what gives them this power. Daily mark-to-market settlement makes losses immediate. Most individuals should understand these instruments, but use them only for specific commercial hedging purposes, not speculation.
